turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 23-929, Velazquez versus Bondi.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: This case poses a question about how to calculate a deadline in immigration law. After lawfully determining that an individual is removable from this country, the government will usually detain and deport him. But sometimes the government has a different option. By a statute, the government may, under certain circumstances, defer detention and deportation for up to 60 days in order to allow the individual to leave the country voluntarily. This case involves a dispute about what the phrase 60 days means in the voluntary departure statute. The government argues that the phrase means an individual has exactly 60 calendar days to leave. The petitioner, Mr. Monsalvo, contends that the statute operates to extend a deadline that falls on a weekend or a legal holiday to the next business day. That dispute may seem like a small one, but if so, it is also a dispute that carries with its serious consequences. If an individual overstays his voluntary departure deadline, even by a day, he faces not just detention and deportation, by law, he also faces fines and a ban preventing him from seeking readmission to the country for a decade. So at least in this corner of the law, every day counts. And in truth, both sides have good arguments about how the voluntary departure statute works. An ordinary reader might understand the phrase, 60 days to mean 60 calendar days as the government suggests, but as petitioner observes in many legal settings, deadlines expressed in terms of a number of days operate in a specialized way. So that a deadline falling on a weekend or a legal holiday rolls over to the next business day. The question before us thus boils down to whether the statute uses the term days in its ordinary sense or its specialized legal sense. To resolve that question, we turn to one of this Court’s traditional interpretive tools. When Congress adopts a new statute against the backdrop of a longstanding administrative construction, this Court has long said, it will generally presume that the new law works in harmony with what came before. And as it turns out, since at least the 1950s, immigration regulations have consistently provided that when calculating the deadline for the taking of any action, the term day carries its specialized meaning. So under our precedents, we presume that the voluntary departure statute works the same way and thus operates to roll over a deadline falling on a weekend or a legal holiday the next business day. Fortifying our usual presumption on this score is another notable statutory clue. Congress adopted the voluntary departure deadline as part of Section 304 of the Illegal Immigration Reform and Immigrant Responsibility Act of 1996. That provision contains a number of deadlines, not just for voluntary departures, but for other things like filing a motion to reopen or to reconsider. For those other deadlines in Section 304, the government concedes the specialized meaning of day applies. And it seems to us that when two provisions in a single section of an act of Congress use the term days in its specialized sense, it is all the more sensible to think a third provision in the same section of the same law does as well. For those reasons and a number of others detailed in our opinion that I will not belabor here, we conclude that the voluntary departure deadline works like so many others in immigration law. Because the Tenth Circuit held otherwise, its judgment is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito has joined, and Justice Kavanaugh and Barrett have joined in part. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kavanaugh has joined. Justice Barrett has also filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kavanaugh has joined.
